caaar.description: Is equivalent to (car (car (car pair))). Returns the result of three-level car nesting on pair.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

caadr.description: Is equivalent to (car (car (cdr pair))). Takes the cdr first, then car twice.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

caar.description: Is equivalent to (car (car pair)). Returns the car of the car of pair.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least two levels deep

cadar.description: Is equivalent to (car (cdr (car pair))). Takes the car first, then cdr, then car.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

caddr.description: Is equivalent to (car (cdr (cdr pair))). Returns the third element of a list.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a list with at least three elements or a nested pair

caddr.example:
(caddr '(a b c d))  => c
(caddr '((a b) (c d) (e f)))  => (e f)

cadr.description: Is equivalent to (car (cdr pair)). Returns the second element of a list.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a list with at least two elements

cdaar.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (car (car pair))). Takes car twice, then cdr.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

cdadr.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (car (cdr pair))). Takes cdr first, then car, then cdr.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

cdar.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (car pair)). Returns the cdr of the car of pair.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least two levels deep

cddar.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (cdr (car pair))). Takes car first, then cdr twice.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a pair nested at least three levels deep

cdddr.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (cdr (cdr pair))). Returns the sublist after removing the first three elements.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a list with at least three elements

cddr.description: Is equivalent to (cdr (cdr pair)). Returns the sublist after removing the first two elements.

parameters.name: pair
parameters.desc: a list with at least two elements

make-polar.description: Constructs a complex number from polar coordinates. Returns r*exp(i*theta). r is the magnitude (a non-negative real number), theta is the angle (in radians).

and.description: Special form (library syntax). Evaluates the test expressions from left to right and returns the value of the first expression that evaluates to a false value (#f). The remaining expressions are not evaluated. If all expressions evaluate to true values, the value of the last expression is returned. If there are no expressions, #t is returned. A short-circuit evaluation form.

apply.description: Procedure. proc must be a procedure and args must be a list. Calls proc with the elements of (append (list arg1 ...) args) as actual arguments.

parameters.name: args
parameters.desc: the last argument must be a list; its elements become the remaining arguments

begin.description: Special form (library syntax). Evaluates the expressions from left to right and returns the value of the last expression. Used for sequencing side-effecting operations (such as input/output).

parameters.name: expr1 expr2 ...
parameters.desc: one or more expressions, evaluated sequentially

call-with-current-continuation.description: Procedure. proc must be a procedure that accepts one argument. Encapsulates the current continuation as an "escape procedure" and passes it to proc. When the escape procedure is called, it abandons the current continuation and uses the continuation that was in effect when the escape procedure was created. The extent of the escape procedure is unlimited; it can be stored and called multiple times at any point.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure that accepts one argument (the continuation)

call-with-values.description: Procedure. Calls producer (a procedure) with a values continuation. When producer returns some values via values, consumer is called with those values as arguments. The continuation of the call to consumer is the continuation of the call to call-with-values.

parameters.name: producer
parameters.desc: a zero-argument producer procedure that returns values via values

parameters.name: consumer
parameters.desc: a consumer procedure that accepts the values returned by producer as arguments

call/cc.description: Procedure. Syntactic sugar for call-with-current-continuation. Encapsulates the current continuation as an escape procedure and passes it to proc. Behavior is identical to call-with-current-continuation.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure that accepts one argument (the continuation)

case.description: Special form (library syntax). Evaluates key and compares the result with each datum using eqv?. If the result matches a datum in some clause, the expressions of that clause are evaluated sequentially and the value of the last expression is returned. If no match is found and an else clause exists, the expressions in the else clause are evaluated. The datums within each clause must be distinct.

parameters.name: key
parameters.desc: the key expression to be matched

parameters.name: ((datum ...) expr1 ...)
parameters.desc: one or more matching clauses; datums are external representations compared using eqv?

case.example:
(case (* 2 3)
  ((2 3 5 7) 'prime)
  ((1 4 6 8 9) 'composite))  => composite
(case (car '(c d))
  ((a e i o u) 'vowel)
  ((w y) 'semivowel)
  (else 'consonant))  => consonant

cond.description: Special form (library syntax). Evaluates the test expressions of each clause from left to right until a test evaluates to a true value. The remaining expressions in that clause are then evaluated sequentially and the value of the last expression is returned. If a clause contains only a test and no expressions, the value of the test is returned. If all tests yield false values and an else clause exists, the else clause is evaluated. Supports the => syntax: (test => proc), where proc is called with the value of the test as its argument. If all tests yield false values and there is no else clause, the result is unspecified.

parameters.name: (test expr1 ...)
parameters.desc: one or more conditional clauses; test is the test expression

parameters.name: (else expr1 ...)
parameters.desc: an optional else clause, which must be the last clause

define.description: Special form. Definition form. The first form binds var to the value of expr. The second form is equivalent to (define var (lambda (args ...) body ...)), which is syntactic sugar for defining a procedure. Also supports the rest argument form (define (var . formal) body ...), which is equivalent to (define var (lambda formal body ...)). May only be used at the top level of a program or at the beginning of a body.

parameters.name: expr
parameters.desc: expression the variable is bound to (first form)

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: procedure body, one or more expressions (second form)

define.example:
(define x 28)
x  => 28
(define add3 (lambda (x) (+ x 3)))
(add3 3)  => 6
(define (square x) (* x x))
(square 5)  => 25

delay.description: Special form (library syntax). Used with the force procedure to implement lazy evaluation (call-by-need). Returns an object called a promise, which will evaluate expression and deliver the result only when forced in the future. The value of the promise is cached (memoized); multiple force calls compute the value only once. The effect of expression returning multiple values is unspecified.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.desc: the expression whose evaluation is delayed

delay.example:
(force (delay (+ 1 2)))  => 3
(define p (delay (begin (display "hi") 1)))
(list (force p) (force p))  => (1 1)，只打印一次 hi

do.description: Special form (library syntax). Iteration construct. Specifies a set of variables, their initial values, and step expressions for each iteration. First, the init expressions are evaluated and the variables are bound. Then iteration begins: test is evaluated; if the result is true, the expr expressions are evaluated sequentially and the value of the last expr is returned (if no expr is present, the return value is unspecified); if the result is false, the body expressions are evaluated sequentially (for side effects), then the step expressions are evaluated to update the variables, and the next iteration begins. The step may be omitted, in which case the variable remains unchanged. The scope of each variable is the entire do expression (excluding the init expressions).

parameters.name: init
parameters.desc: initial value expression for the variable

parameters.name: step
parameters.desc: optional; expression to update the variable after each iteration

parameters.name: expr
parameters.desc: optional; evaluated sequentially when test is true; the value of the last one is returned

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: optional; command expressions evaluated during each iteration

do.example:
(do ((vec (make-vector 5))
     (i 0 (+ i 1)))
    ((= i 5) vec)
  (vector-set! vec i i))  => #(0 1 2 3 4)
(do ((x '(1 3 5 7 9) (cdr x))
     (sum 0 (+ sum (car x))))
    ((null? x) sum))  => 25

dynamic-wind.description: Procedure. Calls thunk (a zero-argument procedure) and returns its result. before and after are also zero-argument procedures, called according to the following rules: before is called whenever execution enters the dynamic extent of the thunk call, and after is called whenever it leaves. The dynamic extent includes normal return from thunk, as well as entry or exit via continuations captured with call-with-current-continuation. Nested dynamic-wind calls follow the correct entry and exit order.

parameters.name: before
parameters.desc: zero-argument procedure called when entering the dynamic extent

parameters.name: thunk
parameters.desc: zero-argument procedure; the body of the dynamic extent

parameters.name: after
parameters.desc: zero-argument procedure called when leaving the dynamic extent

dynamic-wind.example:
(let ((path '())
      (c #f))
  (dynamic-wind
    (lambda () (set! path (cons 'connect path)))
    (lambda ()
      (set! path (cons (call-with-current-continuation
                        (lambda (c0) (set! c c0) 'talk1))
                      path)))
    (lambda () (set! path (cons 'disconnect path))))
  (if (< (length path) 4)
      (c 'talk2)
      (reverse path)))  => (connect talk1 disconnect connect talk2 disconnect)

else.description: Special form (syntax keyword). May only be used in the last clause of a cond or case expression. When all tests in cond yield false values, or when the key in case matches no datum, the expressions in the else clause are evaluated.

eval.description: Procedure. Evaluates expression in the specified environment and returns its value. expression must be a valid Scheme expression represented as data, and environment-specifier must be the value returned by scheme-report-environment, null-environment, or interaction-environment.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.desc: data object representing a Scheme expression

force.description: Library procedure. Forces the evaluation of a promise (created by delay). If the promise has not yet been evaluated, computes and returns the value. The value of the promise is cached (memoized); subsequent force calls return the cached value directly. force and delay are primarily used for lazy evaluation in a functional style.

parameters.name: promise
parameters.desc: a promise object created by delay

force.example:
(force (delay (+ 1 2)))  => 3
(define p (delay (begin (display "once") 1)))
(force p)  => 1，打印 once
(force p)  => 1，不打印

if.description: Special form. Evaluates test; if the result is a true value, evaluates consequent and returns its value; otherwise evaluates alternate (if provided) and returns its value. If test yields a false value and no alternate is provided, the result is unspecified. In Scheme, only #f is a false value; all other values (including 0 and the empty list) are true values.

parameters.name: consequent
parameters.desc: expression evaluated when the test is true

parameters.name: alternate
parameters.desc: optional; expression evaluated when the test is false

lambda.description: Special form. Evaluates to a procedure. Remembers the environment in which the lambda expression was evaluated. When called, extends that environment by binding the formal parameters to new locations with the actual arguments, then evaluates the expressions in body sequentially, returning the value of the last expression. Formal parameters have three forms: a fixed parameter list (x y), a rest argument x (receives all arguments as a list), or a hybrid form (x y . rest) (rest receives the remaining arguments as a list). The same formal parameter may not appear more than once.

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: procedure body, one or more expressions

let.description: Special form (library syntax). Binding construct. First evaluates all init expressions in the current environment (the order of evaluation is unspecified), then binds each var to a new location holding the value of the corresponding init, and evaluates body in the extended environment, returning the value of the last expression. All init expressions are evaluated before any var is bound. Also supports the named let form: (let name ((var init) ...) body ...), where name is bound within body as a recursive procedure. The same var may not appear more than once.

parameters.name: init
parameters.desc: initial value expression for the variable

let*.description: Special form (library syntax). Binding construct. Similar to let, but the bindings are performed sequentially from left to right. The region of each binding is the portion of the let* expression to its right. Thus, the second binding can be evaluated in the environment where the first binding is visible.

parameters.name: init
parameters.desc: initial value expression for the variable; may be evaluated in the environment where preceding bindings are visible

letrec.description: Special form (library syntax). Binding construct. Each var is bound to a new location holding an undefined value. The init expressions are then evaluated in the resulting environment (the order of evaluation is unspecified), each var is assigned the result of the corresponding init, and body is evaluated in the resulting environment. The binding region of each var is the entire letrec expression, allowing the definition of mutually recursive procedures. Important restriction: it must be possible to evaluate each init without referencing the value of any var. The same var may not appear more than once.

parameters.name: init
parameters.desc: initial value expression for the variable; typically a lambda expression

or.description: Special form (library syntax). Evaluates the test expressions from left to right and returns the value of the first expression that evaluates to a true value. The remaining expressions are not evaluated. If all expressions evaluate to false values, the value of the last expression is returned. If there are no expressions, #f is returned. A short-circuit evaluation form.

quasiquote.description: Special form. Similar to quote, but allows unquote (,) and unquote-splicing (,@) within the template to embed evaluated expressions. If the template contains no commas, it is equivalent to quote. The expression following a comma is evaluated and inserted into the result. The expression following ,@ must evaluate to a list, whose elements are spliced into the result. The backquote character ` is syntactic sugar for quasiquote. quasiquotes can be nested; substitution occurs only at the nesting level of the outermost backquote.

parameters.name: qq-template
parameters.desc: quasiquote template, which may contain ,expr and ,@expr

quote.description: Special form. Returns the value of datum without evaluating datum. datum may be the external representation of any Scheme object. 'datum is syntactic sugar for (quote datum). Numeric constants, string constants, character constants, and boolean constants are self-evaluating and need not be quoted.

set!.description: Special form. Evaluates expression and stores the result in the location bound to variable. variable must already be bound in some region containing the set! expression or at the top level. The return value is unspecified.
set!.example:
(define x 2)
(+ x 1)  => 3
(set! x 5)  => 未指定
(+ x 1)  => 6

values.description: Procedure. Delivers all arguments to the current continuation. All continuations except those created by call-with-values accept exactly one value. The effect of passing zero or more values to a continuation that does not accept that number of values is unspecified.

parameters.name: obj ...
parameters.desc: zero or more values to be returned

values.example:
(values 4 5)  => 返回两个值 4 和 5
(call-with-values (lambda () (values 1 2 3)) list)  => (1 2 3)

#f.description: Boolean false value. In conditional expressions, only #f is treated as false; all other values (including 0 and the empty list '()) are treated as true.

#t.description: Boolean true value. Boolean constants are self-evaluating and need not be quoted.

boolean?.description: Tests whether obj is a boolean (#t or #f). Returns #t or #f.

char?.description: Tests whether obj is a character. Returns #t or #f.

eq?.description: The finest equivalence predicate. Tests whether two objects are the same object in memory (pointer equality). For symbols, booleans, the empty list, pairs, and procedures, behavior is consistent with eqv?; for numbers and characters, the result is implementation-dependent. Typically implemented as pointer comparison, which is more efficient than eqv?.

equal?.description: The coarsest equivalence predicate. Recursively compares the contents of pairs, vectors, and strings, and uses eqv? for other objects (numbers, symbols, etc.). Rule of thumb: if two objects print the same, they are generally equal?. Note: may not terminate on cyclic data structures.

eqv?.description: An intermediate-granularity equivalence predicate. Returns #t for symbols, booleans, exact/inexact numbers, characters, the empty list, pairs/vectors/strings at the same storage location, and procedures with the same location tag. It is a compromise between eq? and equal? -- more precise than eq? (guaranteed correct for numbers and characters) and coarser than equal? (does not recursively compare compound structures).

not.description: Logical negation. If obj is #f, returns #t; otherwise returns #f.

symbol?.description: Tests whether obj is a symbol. Returns #t or #f.

list?.description: Returns #t if obj is a proper list, otherwise returns #f. By definition, all proper lists have finite length and terminate with the empty list.
list?.example:
(list? '(a b c))  => #t
(list? '())  => #t
(list? '(a . b))  => #f
(let ((x (list 'a))) (set-cdr! x x) (list? x))  => #f

null?.description: Returns #t if obj is the empty list, otherwise returns #f.

pair?.description: Returns #t if obj is a pair, otherwise returns #f.

complex?.description: Tests whether obj is a complex number. In the R5RS numeric type hierarchy, all numbers are complex numbers (number > complex > real > rational > integer), so (complex? obj) returns #t for any number. Returns #f for non-numbers. In many implementations, complex? is equivalent to number?.

even?.description: Tests whether the integer n is even. Returns #t or #f.

exact?.description: Tests whether z is an exact number. An exact number is one that is derived from exact constants or from exact numbers using only exact operations. For any Scheme number, exactly one of exact? and inexact? is #t. Exactness is orthogonal to the numeric type (integer/real/complex, etc.).

inexact?.description: Tests whether z is an inexact number. Inexactness is contagious: numbers containing a decimal point, exponent, or inexact component are all inexact. For any Scheme number, exactly one of exact? and inexact? is #t.

integer?.description: Tests whether obj is an integer. In the R5RS numeric type hierarchy (number > complex > real > rational > integer), integer is the innermost subtype. For an inexact real number x, (integer? x) is #t if and only if (= x (round x)).

negative?.description: Tests whether the real number x is negative. Returns #t or #f.

number?.description: Tests whether obj is a number. In the R5RS numeric type hierarchy (number > complex > real > rational > integer), this is the outermost type predicate, returning #t for all numbers.

odd?.description: Tests whether the integer n is odd. Returns #t or #f.

positive?.description: Tests whether the real number x is positive. Returns #t or #f.

rational?.description: Tests whether obj is a rational number. In the R5RS numeric type hierarchy (number > complex > real > rational > integer), rational numbers include all integers and fractions. In many implementations, rational? is equivalent to real?, since inexact numbers are typically treated as approximations of rational numbers.

real?.description: Tests whether obj is a real number. In the R5RS numeric type hierarchy (number > complex > real > rational > integer), a real number is a complex number with a zero imaginary part. For an inexact complex number z, (real? z) is #t if and only if (zero? (imag-part z)) is #t.

zero?.description: Tests whether the number z is zero. Returns #t or #f.

procedure?.description: Returns #t if obj is a procedure, otherwise returns #f. Note: the name of a procedure (such as the quoted symbol 'car) is not itself a procedure.

string?.description: Returns #t if obj is a string, otherwise returns #f.

vector?.description: Returns #t if obj is a vector, otherwise returns #f.

eof-object?.description: Procedure. Tests whether obj is an end-of-file object. Returns #t or #f. The specific set of EOF objects is implementation-dependent, but no EOF object can be read in via read.

input-port?.description: Procedure. Tests whether obj is an input port. Returns #t or #f.

output-port?.description: Procedure. Tests whether obj is an output port. Returns #t or #f.

port?.description: Procedure. Tests whether obj is a port (input port or output port). Returns #t or #f. In the R5RS type system, port is a top-level type alongside boolean, pair, symbol, number, char, string, vector, and procedure.

string->symbol.description: Returns the symbol whose name is string. This procedure can create symbols whose names contain special characters or non-standard case letters, but this is generally not recommended, as such symbols may not be readable in some Scheme implementations. It is the inverse of symbol->string.

append.description: Returns a new list consisting of the elements of the list arguments concatenated sequentially. The result list is always newly allocated, except that it shares structure with the last list argument. The last argument may be any object; if it is not a proper list, the result is an improper list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: lists to concatenate; the last argument may be any object

append.example:
(append '(a b) '(c d))  => (a b c d)
(append '(a) '(b c d))  => (a b c d)
(append '() '())  => ()
(append '(a b) '(c . d))  => (a b c . d)
(append '() 'a)  => a

assoc.description: Finds the first pair in the association list alist whose car field is equal to obj, and returns that pair. If no such pair exists, returns #f. Uses equal? for comparison.

parameters.name: alist
parameters.desc: association list; each element must be a pair

assq.description: Finds the first pair in the association list alist whose car field is equal to obj, and returns that pair. If no such pair exists, returns #f. Uses eq? for comparison (pointer/identity equality). Suitable for keys that are atomic values such as symbols.

parameters.name: alist
parameters.desc: association list; each element must be a pair

assv.description: Finds the first pair in the association list alist whose car field is equal to obj, and returns that pair. If no such pair exists, returns #f. Uses eqv? for comparison (value equality). Generally more appropriate for keys that are numbers, characters, or other types with automatic comparison.

parameters.name: alist
parameters.desc: association list; each element must be a pair

car.description: Returns the contents of the car field of pair. It is an error to take the car of the empty list.

cdr.description: Returns the contents of the cdr field of pair. It is an error to take the cdr of the empty list.

cons.description: Returns a newly allocated pair whose car is obj1 and whose cdr is obj2. The pair is guaranteed to be different (in the sense of eqv?) from every existing object.
cons.example:
(cons 'a '())  => (a)
(cons '(a) '(b c d))  => ((a) b c d)
(cons 'a 3)  => (a . 3)
(cons '(a b) 'c)  => ((a b) . c)

for-each.description: The arguments are the same as for map, but for-each calls proc for its side effects rather than for its return value. Unlike map, for-each is guaranteed to call proc on the elements of the lists in order from the first element to the last. The return value is unspecified.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure accepting as many arguments as there are lists

parameters.name: list2
parameters.desc: second list (optional); all lists must have the same length when multiple lists are provided

for-each.example:
(for-each display '(1 2 3))  ; outputs 123
(for-each (lambda (a b) (display (+ a b))) '(1 2 3) '(4 5 6))  ; outputs 6810

length.description: Returns the length of list (the number of elements).

list.description: Returns a newly allocated list containing its arguments. With no arguments, returns the empty list.

list-ref.description: Returns the kth element of list (zero-based index). Equivalent to (car (list-tail list k)). If list has fewer than k+1 elements, an error is signaled.
list-ref.example:
(list-ref '(a b c d) 2)  => c
(list-ref '(a b c d) 0)  => a

list-tail.description: Returns the sublist of list obtained by omitting the first k elements. If list has fewer than k elements, an error is signaled. Equivalent to (define list-tail (lambda (x k) (if (zero? k) x (list-tail (cdr x) (- k 1))))).
list-tail.example:
(list-tail '(a b c d) 2)  => (c d)
(list-tail '(a b c d) 0)  => (a b c d)

map.description: The list arguments must be lists, and proc must be a procedure accepting as many arguments as there are lists and returning a single value. If more than one list is given, they must all have the same length. Map applies proc element-wise to the elements of the lists and returns a list of the results. The dynamic order in which proc is applied to the elements is unspecified.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure accepting as many arguments as there are lists and returning a single value

parameters.name: list2
parameters.desc: second list (optional); all lists must have the same length when multiple lists are provided

member.description: Returns the first sublist of list whose car is equal to obj. If obj does not occur in list, returns #f (not the empty list). Uses equal? for comparison.
member.example:
(member 'a '(a b c))  => (a b c)
(member 'b '(a b c))  => (b c)
(member 'a '(b c d))  => #f
(member '(a) '(b (a) c))  => ((a) c)

memq.description: Returns the first sublist of list whose car is equal to obj. If obj does not occur in list, returns #f (not the empty list). Uses eq? for comparison (pointer/identity equality). Suitable for looking up atomic values such as symbols.
memq.example:
(memq 'a '(a b c))  => (a b c)
(memq 'b '(a b c))  => (b c)
(memq 'a '(b c d))  => #f
(memq '(a) '(b (a) c))  => #f

memv.description: Returns the first sublist of list whose car is equal to obj. If obj does not occur in list, returns #f (not the empty list). Uses eqv? for comparison (value equality). Generally more reliable for keys that are numbers or characters.

reverse.description: Returns a newly allocated list consisting of the elements of list in reverse order.
reverse.example:
(reverse '(a b c))  => (c b a)
(reverse '(a (b c) d (e (f))))  => ((e (f)) d (b c) a)
(reverse '())  => ()

set-car!.description: Stores obj in the car field of pair. The return value is unspecified. This is a mutation operation that modifies the contents of pair.

parameters.name: obj
parameters.desc: new value to store in the car field

set-cdr!.description: Stores obj in the cdr field of pair. The return value is unspecified. This is a mutation operation that modifies the contents of pair. Note: using set-cdr! can turn a proper list into an improper list, or create a cyclic structure.

parameters.name: obj
parameters.desc: new value to store in the cdr field

string-append.description: Returns a newly allocated string whose characters are the concatenation of the given strings in order.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: one or more strings to concatenate

string-length.description: Returns the number of characters in the given string.

vector-length.description: Returns the number of elements in vector as an exact integer.

list->string.description: Returns a newly allocated string consisting of the characters in list. List must be a list of characters. list->string and string->list are inverses in the sense of equal?.

list->vector.description: Returns a newly created vector initialized to the elements of list.

string->list.description: Returns a newly allocated list of the characters of the given string. string->list and list->string are inverses in the sense of equal?.

vector->list.description: Returns a newly allocated list containing all elements of vector.

char->integer.description: Returns the exact integer corresponding to char (its ordinal value / code point). char->integer and integer->char form an order-preserving isomorphism between the character ordering and a subset of the integers.

integer->char.description: Returns the character corresponding to the exact integer n. The value of n must be a valid return value of some call to char->integer. Together with char->integer, forms an order-preserving isomorphism between the character ordering and a subset of the integers.

parameters.name: n
parameters.desc: exact integer (ordinal value of the character)

abs.description: Returns the absolute value of x. x must be a real number.

acos.description: Returns the arccosine of z. The result is in radians. For real arguments, z must be in the range [-1, 1], and the result is in the range [0, pi].

angle.description: Returns the angle (argument) of the complex number z in radians. The result is in the range (-pi, pi]. If z = x + yi = r*e^(i*theta), returns theta. Equivalent to (atan y x).

asin.description: Returns the arcsine of z. The result is in radians. For real arguments, z must be in the range [-1, 1], and the result is in the range [-pi/2, pi/2].

atan.description: Returns the arctangent of the arguments, in radians. The one-argument form (atan y) returns the arctangent of y, with a result in the range (-pi/2, pi/2). The two-argument form (atan y x) returns the angle from the positive x-axis to the point (x, y), with a result in the range (-pi, pi], and is equivalent to (angle (make-rectangular x y)).

ceiling.description: Returns the smallest integer not less than x (rounds toward positive infinity). If the argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

cos.description: Returns the cosine of z, where z is in radians.

exact->inexact.description: Returns an inexact representation of z. The return value is the inexact number numerically closest to the argument. If no reasonably close inexact equivalent exists for the exact argument, an implementation restriction may be reported.

exp.description: Returns e raised to the power z (exponential function).

expt.description: Returns z1 raised to the power z2. For z1 not equal to 0, this is equivalent to e^(z2*log(z1)). 0^0 is 1, and 0^z for z not equal to 0 is 0. When both arguments are exact integers, the result is an exact integer whenever possible.

floor.description: Returns the largest integer not greater than x (rounds toward negative infinity). If the argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

gcd.description: Returns the greatest common divisor of the arguments. The result is always non-negative. With no arguments, returns 0. When all arguments are exact integers, the result is an exact non-negative integer.

imag-part.description: Returns the imaginary part of the complex number z. If z = x + yi, returns y.

lcm.description: Returns the least common multiple of the arguments. The result is always non-negative. With no arguments, returns 1. When any argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

log.description: Returns the natural logarithm of z (base e), not the common logarithm (base 10). The imaginary part of the result is in the range (-pi, pi]. (log 0) is undefined.

magnitude.description: Returns the magnitude (absolute value) of the complex number z. If z = x + yi, returns sqrt(x^2 + y^2). For real arguments, magnitude is equivalent to abs.

make-rectangular.description: Constructs a complex number from rectangular coordinates. Returns x + yi.

max.description: Returns the largest of all arguments. If any argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.desc: real number (rest argument, at least one)

min.description: Returns the smallest of all arguments. If any argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

parameters.name: x1
parameters.desc: real number (rest argument, at least one)

modulo.description: Returns the modulo operation result of n1 divided by n2. n2 must not be zero. The result is an integer satisfying: n1 = n2*q + m, where q is the quotient truncated toward negative infinity, |m| < |n2|, and m has the same sign as n2. The difference from remainder is: the result of remainder has the same sign as the dividend n1, while the result of modulo has the same sign as the divisor n2.

number->string.description: Converts the number num to its string representation. radix must be an exact integer 2, 8, 10, or 16, defaulting to 10. For inexact numbers with radix 10, the result contains a decimal point and uses the minimum number of digits. The result string does not contain an explicit radix prefix.

quotient.description: Returns the integer quotient of n1 divided by n2 (truncated toward zero). n2 must not be zero. Together with modulo and remainder, implements the number-theoretic division: n1 = n2*q + r, where q = (quotient n1 n2) truncated toward zero, and r = (remainder n1 n2) has the same sign as n1.

real-part.description: Returns the real part of the complex number z. If z = x + yi, returns x.

remainder.description: Returns the remainder of n1 divided by n2. n2 must not be zero. The result is an integer satisfying: n1 = n2*q + r, where q = (quotient n1 n2) truncated toward zero, |r| < |n2|, and r has the same sign as n1. The difference from modulo is: the result of remainder has the same sign as the dividend n1, while the result of modulo has the same sign as the divisor n2.

round.description: Returns the integer closest to x (rounding to nearest). When x is exactly halfway between two integers, rounds to the even integer (consistent with the IEEE 754 floating-point default rounding mode). If the argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

sin.description: Returns the sine of z, where z is in radians.

sqrt.description: Returns the principal square root of z. The result has a non-negative real part, or a zero real part and a non-negative imaginary part.

tan.description: Returns the tangent of z, where z is in radians.

truncate.description: Returns the integer obtained by truncating x toward zero. That is, the fractional part is discarded and the integer part is retained. If the argument is inexact, the result is also inexact.

string->number.description: Returns the number of maximum precision represented by the given string. The optional argument radix must be an exact integer 2, 8, 10, or 16, serving as the default radix, which may be overridden by an explicit radix prefix in the string (such as "#o177"). If the string is not a valid representation of a number, returns #f.

symbol->string.description: Returns the name of symbol as a string. If symbol came from a literal or from the read procedure, returns a string in the implementation's preferred case. It is an error to apply mutation procedures such as string-set! to the returned string.

make-string.description: Returns a newly allocated string of length k. If char is supplied, all characters in the string are initialized to char; otherwise the content of the string is unspecified.

string.description: Returns a newly allocated string composed of the given character arguments. This is the constructor for strings.

string-ci=?.description: Returns #t if the two strings are the same length and contain the same characters in corresponding positions (ignoring case), otherwise returns #f.

string-copy.description: Returns a newly allocated copy of the given string.

string-fill!.description: Stores char in every element of the given string. The return value is unspecified. This operation mutates the original string.

string-ref.description: k must be a valid index of string (0 to the length of string minus 1). Uses zero-based indexing to return the kth character of string.

string-set!.description: k must be a valid index of string. Stores char in the kth element of string. The return value is unspecified. This operation mutates the original string.

string=?.description: Returns #t if the two strings are the same length and contain the same characters in corresponding positions, otherwise returns #f. Case-sensitive.

string>=?.description: Compares two strings lexicographically based on character ordering. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically greater than or equal to string2, otherwise returns #f. If the strings differ in length and the shorter string is a prefix of the longer, the longer string is lexicographically greater.

string>?.description: Compares two strings lexicographically based on character ordering. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically strictly greater than string2, otherwise returns #f.

string<=?.description: Compares two strings lexicographically based on character ordering. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically less than or equal to string2, otherwise returns #f.

string<?.description: Compares two strings lexicographically based on character ordering. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically strictly less than string2, otherwise returns #f. The lexicographic ordering is induced by char<?.

string-ci>=?.description: Case-insensitive lexicographic comparison of two strings. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically greater than or equal to string2 when case is ignored, otherwise returns #f.

string-ci>?.description: Case-insensitive lexicographic comparison of two strings. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically strictly greater than string2 when case is ignored, otherwise returns #f.

string-ci<=?.description: Case-insensitive lexicographic comparison of two strings. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically less than or equal to string2 when case is ignored, otherwise returns #f.

string-ci<?.description: Case-insensitive lexicographic comparison of two strings. Returns #t if string1 is lexicographically strictly less than string2 when case is ignored, otherwise returns #f.

char-alphabetic?.description: Tests whether char is an alphabetic character, returning #t or #f. Alphabetic characters include the 52 uppercase and lowercase English letters.

char-ci=?.description: Tests whether two characters are equal, ignoring case. Equivalent to (char=? (char-upcase char1) (char-upcase char2)).

char-downcase.description: Returns the lowercase equivalent of char. If char is an alphabetic character, returns its lowercase form; otherwise returns char itself. The result is guaranteed to satisfy (char-ci=? char result).

char-lower-case?.description: Tests whether char is a lowercase letter, returning #t or #f.

char-numeric?.description: Tests whether char is a numeric character (decimal digit 0-9), returning #t or #f.

char-ready?.description: Tests whether a character is available on the current-input-port. If it returns #t, the next read-char operation is guaranteed not to block. Also returns #t if the port has reached end-of-file. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-input-port.

char-upcase.description: Returns the uppercase equivalent of char. If char is an alphabetic character, returns its uppercase form; otherwise returns char itself. The result is guaranteed to satisfy (char-ci=? char result).

char-upper-case?.description: Tests whether char is an uppercase letter, returning #t or #f.

char-whitespace?.description: Tests whether char is a whitespace character, returning #t or #f. Whitespace characters include space, tab, newline, form feed, and carriage return.

char=?.description: Tests whether two characters are the same. Together with char<?, defines a total ordering on the character set.

char>=?.description: Tests whether char1 is greater than or equal to char2 in character order. Together with char<?, defines a total ordering on the character set.

char>?.description: Tests whether char1 is greater than char2 in character order. Together with char<?, defines a total ordering on the character set.

char<=?.description: Tests whether char1 is less than or equal to char2 in character order. Together with char<?, defines a total ordering on the character set.

char<?.description: Tests whether char1 is less than char2 in character order. Uppercase letters, lowercase letters, and digits are each guaranteed to be in order. The relative ordering between digits and letters is implementation-dependent.

char-ci>=?.description: Tests whether char1 is greater than or equal to char2 in character order, ignoring case.

char-ci>?.description: Tests whether char1 is greater than char2 in character order, ignoring case.

char-ci<=?.description: Tests whether char1 is less than or equal to char2 in character order, ignoring case.

char-ci<?.description: Tests whether char1 is less than char2 in character order, ignoring case. Equivalent to performing char<? after converting both characters to a uniform case.

peek-char.description: Procedure. Returns the next character on the input port without updating the port position (i.e., without consuming the character). If no more characters are available, returns the EOF object. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-input-port. The next read-char or peek-char will return the same character.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional input port, defaults to current-input-port

peek-char.example:
(peek-char)  => returns the next character without consuming it
(let ((c (peek-char)))
  (list c (read-char)))  => e.g. (#\a #\a)

read-char.description: Procedure. Returns the next character on the input port and updates the port position. If no more characters are available, returns the EOF object. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-input-port.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional input port, defaults to current-input-port

write-char.description: Procedure. Writes the character char itself (not its external representation) to the given port. The return value is unspecified. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-output-port.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional output port, defaults to current-output-port

make-vector.description: Returns a newly allocated vector of k elements. If fill is supplied, each element is initialized to fill; otherwise the initial content of each element is unspecified.

vector.description: Returns a newly allocated vector whose elements are the given arguments. Similar to list, but returns a vector instead of a list.

vector-fill!.description: Stores fill in every element of the vector. The return value is unspecified. This operation mutates the original vector.

vector-ref.description: k must be a valid index of vector (0 to the length of vector minus 1). Returns the contents of the kth element of the vector.

vector-set!.description: k must be a valid index of vector. Stores obj in the kth element of the vector. The return value is unspecified. This operation mutates the original vector. Note: vector-set! must not be used on constant vectors.

call-with-input-file.description: Library procedure. string should be a filename string, and proc should be a procedure that accepts one argument. Opens the named file to obtain an input port, and calls proc with that port as its argument. The port is closed after proc returns, and the result value of proc is returned. An error is signaled if the file cannot be opened.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure that accepts one port argument

call-with-output-file.description: Library procedure. string should be a filename string, and proc should be a procedure that accepts one argument. Opens the named file to obtain an output port, and calls proc with that port as its argument. The port is closed after proc returns, and the result value of proc is returned. If the file already exists, the effect is unspecified. An error is signaled if the file cannot be opened.

parameters.name: proc
parameters.desc: procedure that accepts one port argument

close-input-port.description: Procedure. Closes the file associated with port, rendering the port incapable of delivering characters. Has no effect if the file is already closed. The return value is unspecified.

close-output-port.description: Procedure. Closes the file associated with port, rendering the port incapable of accepting characters. Has no effect if the file is already closed. The return value is unspecified.

current-input-port.description: Procedure. Returns the current default input port.

current-output-port.description: Procedure. Returns the current default output port.

display.description: Library procedure. Writes a human-readable representation of obj to the given port. Strings are written without enclosing double quotes, and characters within strings are not escaped. Character objects are output as if by write-char (without the #\ prefix). The return value is unspecified. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-output-port. Unlike write, display is intended for output to be read by people.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional output port, defaults to current-output-port

display.example:
(display "hello")  => prints hello (no quotes)
(display #\A)  => prints A (no #\ prefix)

load.description: Procedure. Reads and evaluates the expressions and definitions from the file specified by filename. filename is a string. load is an extension procedure provided by many Scheme implementations and is not part of the R5RS standard, but is widely used in the SDE environment.

newline.description: Library procedure. Writes a newline character to the given port. The specific implementation varies by operating system. The return value is unspecified. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-output-port.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional output port, defaults to current-output-port

newline.example:
(display "hello")
(newline)  => prints hello followed by a newline

open-input-file.description: Procedure. Takes a string argument that names an existing file, and returns an input port capable of delivering characters from the file. An error is signaled if the file cannot be opened.

open-output-file.description: Procedure. Takes a string argument that names an output file, and returns an output port capable of writing characters to the file. An error is signaled if the file cannot be opened. If a file with the same name already exists, the effect is unspecified.

read.description: Library procedure. Reads the external representation of a Scheme object from the given input port and converts it to the object itself. Returns the next parseable object, updating the port position. If end of file is encountered before any characters constituting an object are found, returns the EOF object. If end of file is encountered in the middle of an object's external representation, an error is signaled. Reading from a closed port is an error. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-input-port.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional input port, defaults to current-input-port

read.example:
(read)  => waits for user input and returns the parsed object
(read (open-input-file "data.scm"))  => reads the first object from the file

read-line.description: Procedure. Reads a line of text from the input port (up to a newline or EOF) and returns it as a string. The newline character is not included in the returned string. If EOF is reached without reading any characters, returns the EOF object. An extension procedure provided by many Scheme implementations, not part of the R5RS standard.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional input port, defaults to current-input-port

read-line.example: (read-line)  => waits for user to input a line and returns it as a string

with-input-from-file.description: Optional library procedure. string should be the name of an existing file, and thunk should be a procedure of no arguments. Opens the file to obtain an input port, sets it as the default for current-input-port, and calls thunk. After thunk returns, the port is closed and the previous default port is restored. Returns the result value of thunk. The behavior when exiting via an escape procedure is implementation-dependent.

with-output-to-file.description: Optional library procedure. string should be a filename string, and thunk should be a procedure of no arguments. Opens the file to obtain an output port, sets it as the default for current-output-port, and calls thunk. After thunk returns, the port is closed and the previous default port is restored. Returns the result value of thunk. If the file already exists, the effect is unspecified. The behavior when exiting via an escape procedure is implementation-dependent.

write.description: Library procedure. Writes a machine-readable representation of obj to the given port. Strings are enclosed in double quotes, and backslashes and double quotes within strings are escaped with backslashes. Character objects use the #\ notation. The return value is unspecified. The port argument may be omitted, defaulting to current-output-port. write is intended for output that can be read back by read.

parameters.name: port
parameters.desc: optional output port, defaults to current-output-port
